title: Getting Started with Managed Agents
summary: Google released a tutorial on building custom agents using Managed Agents in Google AI Studio and the Gemini API. The resource demonstrates how to get started with agent development on Google's platforms.
writeup: Patrick from the Gemini API team demonstrates Google's new managed agents feature, which runs Gemini 2.5 Flash inside a secure, Google-hosted Linux sandbox capable of browsing the web, writing and executing code, and managing files. The tutorial covers both the AI Studio no-code path and the Python SDK path, showing how a single API call can spin up a full agentic environment. Agents are customized through agents.md and skill.md files or a linked GitHub repository, making the pattern portable and version-controllable. The through-line is that heavy multi-step agentic work (fetch data, parse, generate an artifact, iterate) now requires almost no scaffolding from the caller.
key_points: Managed agents run in a Google-hosted secure Linux sandbox where the agent can reason, execute arbitrary code, browse the web, and manage files, all without the developer provisioning any infrastructure.

The Python SDK entry point is client.interactions.create, specifying the agent ID, the user input, and environment='remote'; the Interactions API is optimized for multi-turn sessions by passing the previous interaction_id and environment_id to continue in the same sandbox.

Agents are customized by injecting sources into the agent definition: an agents.md file for system-level instructions, a skill.md file for named capabilities, inline scripts, cloud storage URLs, or a full GitHub repository, all configurable in both AI Studio and via client.agents.create in the API.
File retrieval from the sandbox is not yet supported in the Python SDK and requires a plain REST GET request that returns a tar archive of all files the agent produced in the session.

Streaming is available on the Interactions API, letting callers receive step-by-step agent actions as they happen rather than waiting for a final result, which enables more interactive UI experiences.

The weather dashboard demo illustrates the end-to-end loop: the agent performed a Google search, wrote a Python scraper, parsed the data, and produced an interactive Tailwind CSS HTML dashboard, all from a single natural-language prompt.
